title: Oil discovered in Ogoniland
body: Shell-BP begins oil extraction in Ogoniland. The Ogoni people see none of the revenue. Environmental degradation begins immediately and compounds over subsequent decades.

title: MOSOP founded — the Ogoni Bill of Rights
body: Ken Saro-Wiwa co-founds the Movement for the Survival of the Ogoni People (MOSOP). The Ogoni Bill of Rights is presented to the Nigerian government and international community, demanding political autonomy, environmental remediation, and oil revenue sharing.

title: Ogoni Day — 300,000 march
body: On 4 January, an estimated 300,000 Ogoni people march peacefully in what becomes known as Ogoni Day — one of the largest organised demonstrations in Nigerian history. Shell withdraws its workers from Ogoniland. The Nigerian military government responds with the deployment of the Rivers State Internal Security Task Force.

title: Military repression of Ogoniland
body: Lt Col Paul Okuntimo's Task Force conducts raids, killings, and systematic human rights abuses against Ogoni communities. Hundreds of Ogoni people are killed. Ken Saro-Wiwa is briefly detained and released.

title: Arrests following the Giokoo killings
body: On 21 May 1994, four Ogoni chiefs are killed at a pro-government meeting in Giokoo. Ken Saro-Wiwa, who was not present, is arrested along with eight other MOSOP leaders. All are charged with incitement to murder. Human rights organisations immediately question the charges.

title: The tribunal and the executions
body: A military-appointed tribunal tries the nine men. Defence lawyers are harassed. A key prosecution witness later recants, claiming he was bribed. The international community calls for clemency. On 10 November 1995, Ken Saro-Wiwa, Saturday Dobee, Nordu Eawo, Daniel Gbooko, Paul Levura, Felix Nuate, Baribor Bera, Barinem Kiobel, and John Kpuinen are executed by hanging at Port Harcourt Prison.

title: International response — Commonwealth suspension
body: Nigeria is suspended from the Commonwealth of Nations. The United States, European Union, and many governments impose sanctions or issue condemnations. Shell faces sustained international criticism for its complicity, real or perceived, in the Nigerian government's actions.

title: Death of Sani Abacha
body: Military dictator Sani Abacha dies in office. Democratic transition begins in Nigeria. The Ogoni Nine case becomes a central reference point in discussions of corporate accountability, environmental justice, and the rights of minority peoples under authoritarian rule.

title: Shell settlement — Wiwa v. Royal Dutch Shell
body: Shell agrees to pay $15.5 million to settle a lawsuit brought in the United States by the families of Ken Saro-Wiwa and other victims of the Nigerian government's actions. Shell denies wrongdoing. The settlement does not include an admission of liability.

title: UNEP report on Ogoniland
body: The United Nations Environment Programme publishes its landmark Environmental Assessment of Ogoniland, describing hydro-carbon pollution affecting land, water, and air across the region. It recommends a $1 billion clean-up fund and estimates full environmental remediation could take 25–30 years.

title: Hydrocarbon Pollution Remediation Project (HYPREP) launched
body: The Nigerian government launches HYPREP to begin implementing the UNEP recommendations. Progress has been criticised as slow. Ogoni communities continue to live with contaminated soil and water.

title: Posthumous pardon by President Tinubu
body: On 10 November 2023 — the 28th anniversary of the executions — Nigerian President Bola Ahmed Tinubu grants posthumous pardons to Ken Saro-Wiwa and the eight other executed activists. Saro-Wiwa's family and supporters welcome the gesture while calling for full legal exoneration and continued accountability for the environmental destruction of Ogoniland.
